author: @ayanfevese
text:
This comment section makes me sorrowful for the future of this nation. 
I'd encourage you all to cross examine how you treat women in this society. A woman talks, she's shamed and asked to bring "evidence". She doesn’t talk immediately but holds it in and tries to move on, "why is it just now she's talking? Why didn’t she tell her husband? Hehh she's lying oh!" 
Check your own reactions in various cases of abuse or harassment, notice how no matter what, the woman is blamed, one way or another. 
I want you to see how women can’t win and how we're consistently held accountable for actions that are not our own. Sen Natasha had nothing to gain coming out and everything to lose, as has been made clear by the fallout of this.
I want you to see that you don't need a victim to be 'perfect' in order to stand for justice in the case brought forward. You're not perfect, but does that mean as a human being you don’t deserve justice?
And demanding 'evidence' from a victim is just darned ridiculous. If you truly wanted evidence, you would not be holding the victim accountable, you would be holding the institution she complained to, the system that shut down on arrival any hope of an investigation being done that would bring out the "evidence" you so desire, accountable and not the victim. 

Sometimes, some behaviours i see in Nigeria bring me much shame.

Nigeria, open your eyes. Wake up from your slumber! Don’t let a broken system get the best of you.

author: @DaudaAhmed-f3o
text:
Below are the type of bills Nigeria need right but nobody in the National Assembly will sponsor the bill:

1). The bill to prohibits all political office holders/public servants and their families from accessing health care and education outside the country.

2). There should be a bill to establish a special court for corruption. Such a court must conclude all matters within 90 days.

3). A bill where people with ongoing corruption cases cannot be allowed to contest elections until their cases are concluded.

4). A bill for life imprisonment for corrupt public officials without an option of fine.

5). A bill to remove power from the president to appoint INEC chairman, EFCC Chairman, Police IG and Chief Justice of Nigeria.

6). A bill to criminalise unkept election campaign promises. It should be treated as Advanced Votes Fraud.

7). A bill that makes it compulsory for all 774 LGAs in the country, to cultivate at least 300 acres of farm to boost and sustain food production.

text: I dare say that everything the Senator has said on this platform, in relation to the circumstances of her suspension, is false, malicious and denigrating to the Nigerian Senate. By hindsight, l wish to say that l watched all that transpired on the days that the whole drama on the floor of the Senate, from when Senator Natasha brought up her allegation of sexual harassment, till when she was suspended. Her suspension was basically on her rudeness, insubordination, lack of decorum and a clear ignorance of the rules of the Senate. For fairness, she was expected to appear before a Panel of Ethics to state her own side on the allegations of rudeness and flagrant disregard to Senate rules on seating arrangement, but she blatantly refused to turn up. As a woman, the Senator does not portray herself as a responsible woman. She seems to be restless, and never composed, suggesting that she may be under one influence or the other. Even in dressing, she loses up her head gear uncontrollably. She needs to be well-schooled to be a typical Nigerian woman, on the issues of etiquette and moral values.

text:
Ayo was spot on with me on the example of a young daughter reporting a sexual harassment case and the consequences of refusing, by her boss/superior officer to her dad. As a young lady fresh out of college, I had such an experience from an overall male boss. He kept on pestering and threatening me, sending older women to convince me and promising me heaven on earth if only I accepted his promiscuous and nonsensical sexual advances. I  reported the matter to my Dad when I couldn't hold back anymore. I remember that my Dad took the matter up with the chairman of the board of directors. Nothing was done about it, apart from the chairman apologising on the man's behalf. The next best thing I could do then was to look for another job elsewhere. The alternative for me was to resign and stay at home. 

Senator Natasha Akpoti-Uduaghan must have been pushed to the wall, most likely, haven't been made to suffer the consequences of refusal severally on the floor of the house, as she stated, by a fellow senator who happened to be just a first amongst equals by an in-house vote. 

Since not all Nigerian men are like that (these are  about 5 - 10%), the sane minority groups should join forces with the majority of Nigerian women who abhor such crime to say "No" to these randy pigs, and work against all odds to eradicate this societal nuisance,  legally. This injustice must not be allowed to continue.

author: @adelabuolumide6958
text:
Sometimes I wonder how some Nigerians think. Ignorance is indeed a problem in this country. Apart from the fact that Natasha didn't  present the case the way it happened, IPU/UN too have rules and guidelines for their proceedings, should those rules be violated at will? . Was Natasha suspended without reasons? She is mixing things up bcos she wanted people to believe her story. She was suspended for violating rules of the house repeatedly and without showing remorse and not for presenting Petition on sexual harrassment.I'm sure UN/IPU are not stupid too to jump into conclusion. They will do their findings too anyway. Apart from that, Nigeria is a sovereign state, UN/IPU cannot interfere in her affairs. UN/IPU have no power or  right to do anything about this case, they can only mediate, advice or plead on her behalf, they cannot exert any authority on the Senate.Senate is an institution of government in Nigeria, it has its own rules guaranteed by the Constitution of the federal republic of Nigeria.

author: Exola Blog
text:
Kogi Central Watches as Natasha Makes Her Opening Move for Another Term in Nigeria 

In today’s Nigerian politics where paperwork is treated like a declaration of destiny, Natasha Akpoti-Uduaghan arrived at the national secretariat of the Peoples Democratic Party in Abuja with supporters, confidence, and the kind of outfit that silently says this is not just form submission but a soft launch for 2027, because in Nigeria even walking into a building can double as a campaign rally when the cameras are present and the energy is correct.

By submitting her senatorial nomination forms, she did not merely signal interest in re-election for Kogi Central, she activated the long familiar ritual where Nigerian politicians announce the future years before the future arrives, while voters are still trying to survive the present, and yet everyone understands that this is how the dance begins long before the music starts playing.

She thanked her people, praised democratic values, and spoke about service, inclusion, youth empowerment, women empowerment, infrastructure, healthcare, education, and economic opportunities in one smooth political sentence that managed to cover every manifesto keyword without pausing for breath, which is impressive because Nigerian political vocabulary has now become a genre of literature on its own.

author: TheCable
text:
‘We’ve started a movement of hope’ — Natasha Akpoti submits PDP nomination forms for Kogi senate race

Natasha Akpoti-Uduaghan, senator representing Kogi central, has submitted her Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) nomination forms ahead of the 2027 elections.

The lawmaker, accompanied by supporters and political associates, submitted her forms at the national secretariat of the PDP in Abuja on Monday.

Addressing her supporters after submitting the forms, Akpoti-Uduaghan thanked residents of Kogi central and PDP members for their continued support.

https://t.co/wGkCunqBqz

author: Aikon
text:
Natasha Akpoti-Uduaghan is in the PDP. Within just about two years in office, she delivered more visible impact to her people than many politicians do in an entire tenure.

From the provision of transformers, water projects and boreholes, to the distribution of tricycles, sewing and grinding machines, fertilizers, grains, business support initiatives, medical outreach programmes, youth skill acquisition, and even 5,000 digital learning devices for public schools, she has consistently demonstrated what people-focused representation should look like. 

This is why elections should not be reduced to blind party loyalty. The real question should always be: who has the competence, courage, compassion, capacity, and tested integrity to genuinely serve the people?

Vote competence. Vote proven capacity. Vote tested integrity.

author: E.P Aninze Esq.
text:
So yesterday, Adams Oshiomhole flared up at the floor of the Senate in a manner that would have attracted the kind of Natasha Akpoti treatment if he was not someone with an old political profile and deep political roots.

The debate became heated because Godswill Akpabio decided to behave like Machiavelli, amending the Senate rules in a way that would ensure that he and a few of his ring gangs remain the only ones eligible for principal elective positions in the next 11th Assembly.

 Akpabio-led Senate amended the 2023 Senate Standing Rules to favour only senators with at least two consecutive terms, eight uninterrupted years in the immediate preceding Assembly, making them eligible to contest for presiding positions such as Senate President, Deputy Senate President and other principal offices.

To Oshiomhole, it was not just undemocratic. It was autocratic at the same time. That was where the rage came from.

But my real problem in all of this is the seriousness, energy and aggression that Oshiomhole and many others in the Senate suddenly display anytime a debate concerns their welfare, positions or personal political interests.

Today, despite the biting national food crisis confronting millions of Nigerians, bills in that sector account for only 7.3% of the Senate’s total legislative output.

With the frightening level of insecurity across Nigeria, security-related bills make up just 5.4% of the Senate’s agenda.

Critical initiatives like the National Ranches Commission Establishment Bill 2024 have continued to suffer delays, controversies and endless political drama instead of constructive resolution.

Even the Electoral Act Amendment Bill 2026 faced intense public backlash.

But in all these debates, you would almost think people like Oshiomhole had lost their voices completely. The passion disappears. The fire goes cold. The urgency vanishes.

Yet, bring a topic that touches their interests, positions, welfare or political future, and they will almost pull down the walls of the Senate trying to protect it.

At that moment, you will understand that for many of them, it was never really about the people. It has always been about the elite protecting the elite.

I am Ekene Aninze Esq.

author: Ike
text_clean: - Senator NatashaAkpotis latest Facebook post dismissing Dr. Sandra Durus aka Prof. Mgbeke May 1 live broadcast as manipulated and politically motivated offers no facts, no forensics, and certainly no credibility. What it does offer, unfortunately, is a continuation of a now familiar pattern sensational allegations, quickly recycled into conspiracy claims once confronted with inconvenient evidence.Dr. Durus two-hour livestreamcomplete with audio recordings and screenshotsdirectly contradicted the Senators repeated denials. These materials, if authentic, expose a troubling consistency a public official weaponizing unproven accusations for political mileage, then falling back on deflection once the narrative begins to unravel.To date, Senator Akpoti-Uduaghan has claimed, without presenting a single verifiable document, that1. Senate President Godswill Akpabio sexually harassed her2. Former Governor Yahaya Bello was contracted by Akpabio to assassinate her3. The Akpabios were involved in the 2021 death of a minor4. Her reassignment in the Senate seating plan was punishment for rejecting advances5. Her motions and bills are being blocked on the Senate floor6. She has been denied official travel approvals and now self-sponsors her statutory assignments7. And now, most recently, that all incriminating recordings are part of a grand Akpabio script read aloud by a woman she insists is a fraud.Not one of these claims has been backed by a police report, a sworn affidavit, a Senate motion, or even a single authenticated document. And when confronted with contradictory evidence, the Senators response has been reflexive attack the source, dismiss the proof, shift the narrative.Let it be said plainlyNigerias public institutions are not theatres for trial-by-sentiment. When elected leaders make explosive allegations, they owe the public something more than emotive storytelling. They owe facts.At this point, four basic, non-negotiable steps remain1. Authenticate or refute Dr. Durus recordings through an independent forensic labpublicly, not via hashtags or captions.2. Present the evidencepolice reports, court filings, Senate records, audio verificationsthat support your claims.3. If you believe youve been defamed, file suit. Nigeria has courts, not just cameras.4. Failing the above, tender a public apology and resign honorably.The integrity of Nigerias democratic institutions depends on the honesty and accountability of those who serve within them. In the interest of promoting accountability and restoring confidence in our national discourse, it is long overdue for Senator Natasha Akpoti-Uduaghan to stop hiding behind rhetoric and start supplying proof.Nigerians deserve better than conjecture and drama. The time for storytelling has expired. author: Masu Zafi 🔥🔥
text:
THE 3 POINTS AGENDA:
Explosive Allegations Rock Nigeria's Senate: Sister-in-Law Demands Godswill Akpabio's Resignation Amid Claims of Betrayal and Abuse.

LAGOS, Nigeria, October 18, 2025.
In a dramatic escalation of familial and political tensions, Mrs. Pat Akpabio, the wife of Senate President Godswill Akpabio's brother Ibanga, has publicly accused the nation's third-highest-ranking official of abuse of power, betrayal of family members, and a pattern of victimizing women. In a nearly five-minute video recorded from inside a car and shared widely on social media platforms like X (formerly Twitter), Mrs. Akpabio vows to expose more damaging scandals unless the Senate President steps down immediately.

The video, which surfaced on October 16 and has garnered over 240,000 views on a single X post alone, captures Mrs. Akpabio in a passionate rant. "I've never seen a man who is so intoxicated with power. You victimize women! Why are you scared of powerful women?" she declares, her voice laced with frustration. 

She further alleges that Akpabio attempted to imprison his own brother, her husband, in an Abuja prison, and accuses him of being a "sadist," "narcissist," and "blackmailer" who betrays those closest to him. "You were a cab driver... You are nobody," she says, referencing his pre-political life and claiming he has forgotten those who helped him rise to prominence. Mrs. Akpabio also links him to unresolved killings during his tenure as governor of Akwa Ibom State and as a senator, stating, "For all the people you people have killed when you were the governor... and those you people have killed now that you are in the Senate, it will never be well with you."

She concludes with an ultimatum: "The only way you will get me not to expose you is to vacate the Senate. If not, this is just the beginning." The post sharing the video has sparked 398 replies and counting, amplifying calls for accountability within President Bola Tinubu's All Progressives Congress (APC) administration.

2.  A Pattern of Controversy: Echoes of Past Allegations
These accusations do not emerge in isolation. They echo a series of 2025 claims against Akpabio, particularly those involving the alleged targeting of influential women. In March, Senator Natasha Akpoti-Uduaghan, representing Kogi Central, accused Akpabio of sexual harassment, abuse of office, and obstruction of her legislative duties. The Nigerian Senate responded by suspending her for six months, a move criticized by international observers as a "classic abuse of power." Akpoti-Uduaghan, in a televised interview, vowed to present evidence of the assault, but the Senate's ethics committee dismissed her petition. The incident drew global attention, with the BBC reporting on the suspension and highlighting concerns over gender dynamics in Nigerian politics.

Similarly, allegations from a Rivers State woman have resurfaced in public discourse. Former Niger Delta Development Commission (NDDC) Managing Director Joy Nunieh accused Akpabio of harassment in 2020, claims that gained renewed traction in 2025 amid broader scrutiny of his conduct. Former Vice President Atiku Abubakar weighed in, labeling Akpabio a "corrupt leader with a history of abusing women" during his governorship. These incidents suggest a recurring pattern, fueling online criticism of Akpabio's character and raising questions about his fitness for office.

3.  Akpabio's Dismissal and the Broader Political Fallout
In response, Senate President Akpabio, through his media aide, dismissed the video as an "attention-seeking outburst" from a "disgruntled relative" incapable of tarnishing his reputation. On October 17, he stated that the allegations were baseless and urged the public to focus on his legislative achievements rather than family disputes. Akpabio's camp has portrayed the rant as motivated by personal grievances, possibly linked to unmet expectations or internal family conflicts.

"The River state woman said it about him. Natasha said it about him. His brother's wife is saying it!"

Yes, we still have people who express skepticism, suggesting Mrs. Akpabio's motives stem from exclusion from political benefits.

As Nigeria grapples with economic challenges and political instability, this family feud underscores deeper concerns about power, gender, and ethics in governance. 

While the allegations remain unproven, they have undeniably amplified scrutiny on one of the country's most powerful figures. 

Mrs. Akpabio has hinted at more revelations, leaving the nation watching to see if this is indeed "just the beginning."

author: CABS WORLD TV
text:
Trial of Senator Natasha Akpoti-Uduaghan on Cybercrime Charges Stalled

Photo Credit: X (Formerly Twitter)

The planned commencement of the trial on cybercrime charges against suspended Kogi Central Senator Natasha Akpoti-Uduaghan at the Federal High Court in Abuja was stalled following an objection raised by her lead counsel, Ehiogie West-Idahosa, SAN. The objection led the presiding judge to defer the matter, ordering that the court address the objection before proceeding. The trial has now been rescheduled to begin on October 20, 2025.

The charges against Senator Akpoti-Uduaghan relate to alleged cybercrime activities, which stem from her legal disputes with Senate President Godswill Akpabio and former Governor Yahaya Bello. The case is part of a wider confrontation in the National Assembly and involves additional accusations such as defamation.

Senator Akpoti-Uduaghan, who has continuously denied wrongdoing, appeared in court accompanied by her legal team. Her counsel successfully argued that certain procedural issues needed resolution before the trial could officially start. The Federal Government’s lawyer disagreed, insisting that the objection was insufficient to halt the trial, but the judge ruled in favor of hearing the objection.

The case is being closely followed as it highlights tensions within the Nigerian political landscape, especially amid accusations of political marginalization and legal battles involving high-profile figures. Earlier court rulings granted bail to Senator Akpoti-Uduaghan, with conditions set by the court.

As the next court date approaches, legal teams are preparing to present evidence and arguments, with significant attention on how the trial outcomes might impact the senator's political career and broader legislative dynamics in Nigeria.

Sources: BBC Pidgin, Punch Nigeria, Vanguard, Guardian Nigeria, Royal News, Matrix Nigeria

⚠️ NATIONAL SECURITY ALERT ⚠️

FROM: Prof. MGBEKE (Dr. Sandra Duru)
TO: NSA • DG-SSS • IGP • PRESIDENCY • NIGERIAN CITIZENS

After courageously revealing a fragment of damning evidence, specifically where Natasha Akpoti recklessly invoked the name of the National Security Adviser (NSA) of Nigeria, exposing her plot to incite public dissent and manipulate national sentiment in her favor, I, Dr. Sandra C. Duru, one of the principal complainants and key witnesses, have now become the target of an aggressive and cowardly campaign of harassment and intimidation. 

I will soon share, in Natasha Akpoti’s own words, the real reason she fled Nigeria, right when she was due to be served with a criminal summons. Instead of facing justice, she ran to America where she began plotting malicious attacks against me and my children. But we give all glory to God and to the strength of the American legal system. Despite all her lies, manipulations, slander, misinformation, and smear campaigns spread through Adeola Fayehun’s platform and various blogs, the truth has stood firm.

Let it be known! I have a clean record. I am not what they claim. And no amount of wicked propaganda will change the facts or silence me. Justice will prevail. Lies can’t stand forever.

Rather than owning up to the chaos she has caused across the Nigerian Senate, ARISE TV, Channels, BBC, Adeola Fayehun’s platform, and multiple social media outlets, or better still presenting verifiable evidence in court where this matter is already being filed by the Attorney General of the Federation (AGF) following a thorough investigation, Natasha Akpoti continues to evade justice and manipulate the public.

Let the record show: when court officials attempted to serve her, she fled Nigeria, only to reappear in the United States, then later resurfaced in the UK, all while orchestrating threats and attacks on me from abroad. I have receipts, timelines, and full documentation on her international movements and sinister plans those will be unveiled in due course. I will do everything to protect my family, my children, and my beautiful name!

Following my partial disclosure of how she tried to weaponize the name of the NSA to destabilize trust in the Nigerian government, sponsored social media operatives began circulating laughable “damage control” narratives, including claims of “AI” and “voice cloning.” Even Adeola Fayehun, whom one would expect to act with better judgment, has chosen to cry over spilled milk, invoking “voice cloning” in a matter far beyond such childish excuses.

Let’s be clear: THIS IS NOT AI. THIS IS NOT A CLONE. THIS IS HER VOICE.

I now issue this direct challenge to the integrity, competence, and accountability of Nigeria’s top 3 security offices:

 1. To the NSA, DG-SSS, and the Inspector General of Police (IGP) — You are hereby urged to immediately guarantee the safety and protection of every journalist and whistleblower who is being threatened by Natasha Akpoti and her associate, Emmanuel.
 2. Nigerians deserve clarity and assurance: Is the Presidency truly safe? How was such classified intelligence carelessly mentioned in a conversation now widely available or did she lie? Is Natasha Akpoti above the Nigerian state?

I have walked this path before with Dr. John Ndanusa Akanya and A.Z. Gadzama, and I’m ready to clean this up again with brilliance and integrity, pro bono, because I love this country but had no choice but to excuse myself for the best.

To the NSA:
You must now address the nation. You owe Nigerians an answer. Is that Natasha Akpoti’s real voice, or not? You’ve heard it. We’ve heard it. The world is watching. It is time to stop shielding criminal recklessness and start defending the integrity of our democracy.

This is bigger than politics. This is treasonous. This is a national disgrace.

We cannot continue to allow individuals to turn citizens against their government using …

Find the full article on my Facebook page: Prof. Mgbeke

This is a global powerhouse, a renowed media scientist, a criminologist, a public relations genius, and a lioness cloaked in divine assignment. But you are free to underestimate her as a jollof rice chef and local village woman, its her signature!

Dr. Sandra C. Duru does not deal in satire. She is surgical, legal, and prophetic. She is Deborah of our generation, and she is not afraid of your marine spirits, your fake feminists, your petty politics, or your blood-stained sponsors.

This is a message to every traitor, every enabler, and every coward hiding behind the false banner of justice while plotting the downfall of a nation: YOUR TAGGED “YORUBA GOVERNMENT” 🇳🇬 will rise above your schemes…..(1/2)

author: Exola Blog
text:
THE ALLEGATIONS.

Natasha Akpoti-Uduaghan, one of only four female senators in a 109-member Senate, didn’t mince words. She’s accused Godswill Akpabio, the Senate President and one of Nigeria’s most powerful men, of making persistent sexual advances toward her. She’s talked about crude remarks about her body that no one should have to endure, let alone a colleague in a professional setting. She’s alleged that when she rejected him, the retaliation was swift and brutal: a six-month suspension without pay, pushed through by a Senate that seemed more interested in protecting its own than investigating the truth. Natasha took her story public, first on Arise TV, then to the BBC and even the Inter-Parliamentary Union. Her voice has pierced through the noise, and the world is listening.

This isn’t just gossip fodder for Lagos tabloids. These are serious claims that strike at the heart of power dynamics in Nigeria. If true, they paint a picture of a Senate President who sees his position as a license to prey, and a system that punishes those who dare say no. For a country already battling a reputation for corruption and governance failures, this is a gut punch we didn’t need.

author: GoldMyneTV
text:
Akpabio said ‘my waist’ would make ‘good movements’, Natasha Akpoti alleges 

Natasha Akpoti-Uduaghan, senator representing Kogi central in the upper legislative chamber, has alleged that Senate President Godswill Akpabio repeatedly threw suggestive and lewd comments her way.

Akpoti-Uduaghan was suspended from the senate on March 6 for “gross misconduct and unruly behaviour” over her seating arrangement altercation with the senate president.

Her suspension came a day after she submitted a sexual harassment petition against Akpabio on the floor of the senate.

In an interview with the BBC, Akpoti-Uduaghan restated that the “gross misconduct” premise for her suspension was a smokescreen to silence her.

She said Akpabio frequently made sexual advances towards her in and out of the senate chamber — and sometimes before other senators.

She alleged that Akpabio would “squeeze my hands in a very suggestive way”.

“There was a time I forgot to wear my ring because I rushed to work. And there were about five senators there. And Akpabio said ‘oh Natasha, you are not wearing your ring… is this an invitation to treat?” she said.

“There was another time he made a statement like ‘Natasha, your husband is really enjoying. It looks like you’d be able to make good movements with your waist’.

He makes such sexist statements. And then they (other senators) all laughed.”

Akpoti-Uduaghan broke down in tears when the interviewer asked what impact her victim shaming and intimidation claims have had on her.

“I just want a place where I would just work. I thought the worst was over, but I guess for a woman, it’s never really over, right?”

She, however, added that the positive for her is that some female parliamentarians from across Africa have been ringing her up to encourage her and to say they face similar sexually charged, male dominated environments in their respective parliaments.

author: Diamond Media
text:
'NIGERIAN SENATE IS RUN LIKE A CULT', SUSPENDED MP TELLS BBC 

A Nigerian senator who alleged she was sexually harassed by one of the country's top politicians has told the BBC the Senate operates like a "cult".

A fortnight ago, Natasha Akpoti-Uduaghan was suspended for six months after submitting a petition saying she had been harassed by Senate President Godswill Akpabio, which he denies.

His office also rejected the cult comparison - the latest twist in a row that has gripped Nigeria, raising questions about gender equality in the socially conservative nation.

Akpoti-Uduaghan said she was being punished for speaking out against the system and that she now feared for her and her two-year-old's safety as her security detail had been stripped.

"The Nigerian Senate operates like a cult. The Senate president runs the Senate like a dictator, not a democrat. There is no freedom of speech, there is no freedom of expression and anyone who dares to go against him gets cut to size," the 45-year-old said in the first TV interview since her suspension.

In a statement to the BBC, the Senate's Deputy Chief Whip Onyekachi Nwebonyi said Senator Akpoti-Uduaghan was not being silenced and that "her own legislative activities disprove this claim".

Akpoti-Uduaghan, who represents the opposition Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), alleged that the sexual harassment started in 2023, when she visited Akpabio's country home with her husband.

She said the Akpabio "squeezed her hand suggestively" and said "I will make an opportunity for us to come here and have a good moment".

Nwebonyi, who says he was also present during the visit, stated: "At no time during the period in question did the Senate president make any unwanted sexual advances to Senator Akpoti at his country house."

But Akpoti-Uduaghan alleged that this was just one incident of many and that the harassment continued for several months.

She said that, while with other senators on the Senate's premises, Akpabio made a comment to the effect of: "Natasha your husband is really enjoying, it looks like you'll be able to make good movements with your waist."

"The Senate president said this," Akpoti-Uduaghan told the BBC.

"He makes very sexist statements then they [senators] all laugh. He says in the Senate we're all men, she should be used to this."

On his part, Nwebonyi said these allegations were "completely false".

Nigeria has one of the lowest numbers of female parliamentarians in Africa.

Akpoti-Uduaghan one of four female senators in a parliament of 109 members and she is also the first female senator in her state.

Her petition against Akpabio was dismissed due to "procedural errors".

Then, on 6 March, the ethics committee handed Akpoti-Uduaghan a six-month suspension without pay, citing "unruly and disruptive" behaviour during a debate in the Senate.

Akpoti-Uduaghan told the BBC that she no longer had access to security as a result of the suspension.

"In the Nigerian space, we have had people over the past decades who have been killed for having strong views against the government," she said.

"If I'm taking my kid for a walk - because I have a two-year-old - will I be killed? Will I be attacked?"

Civil society groups in the country have expressed concerns over Akpoti-Uduaghan's treatment, calling for a transparent investigation into her allegations.

"All we can say is that today is a sad day for Nigerian women fighting for emancipation. Out of 109 senators, only four are women and one is now suspended," women's rights activist Hadiza Ado told the BBC when the suspension was imposed.

While rights groups and some social media users have expressed their support for Akpoti-Uduaghan, she also has her fair share of detractors.

After she was suspended, two groups of protesters gathered near parliament in the capital, Abuja - one backing the senate president and the other in support of his colleague, chanting ''Akpabio must go''.

Akpoti-Uduaghan had also been subjected to an onslaught of misogynist abuse on social media since making the allegations, she told the BBC.

The scandal is the latest episode in a political career that has been rocky from the outset.

Akpoti-Uduaghan first ran for governor of Kogi state in 2019 and alleged that her candidacy was challenged over her mixed-race heritage.

She eventually appeared on the ballot, but was subjected to physical and verbal attacks during her campaign.

In 2023, she ran for Senate and lost the election. The poll was marred by violence and after Akpoti-Uduaghan took the matter to court, the election result was overturned.

"I thought the worst was over with the elections," she told the BBC.

"But for a woman I guess it's never really over right? It's just different parts, different chapters."

Akpoti-Uduaghan has taken legal action in an attempt to have her suspension overturned and plans to petition the Senate again with her sexual harassment allegations.

Despite the heavy emotional toll, she said she did not regret speaking out.

She said she feels encouraged by her husband, child and the masses of supporters outside her family, which include female politicians from other African countries.

"I have gotten calls on a daily basis from women connecting with me and telling me they are experiencing the same thing in their parliament, but that they can't speak up," she said.

"They say, Natasha, do this for us. This is my story and my story is that of many women in Nigeria that do not have the courage to speak up." [BBC News]
